title-group.article-title: Statin therapy and LDL cholesterol reduction in adults at moderate cardiovascular risk.

license.license-p: This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License.

abstract.p: Statins lower low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol by inhibiting HMG-CoA reductase. This synthetic study evaluated the magnitude of LDL reduction and its tolerability in adults at moderate cardiovascular risk.
Across the cohort, moderate-intensity statin therapy reduced LDL cholesterol by roughly 35 percent from baseline, with most adverse effects limited to mild, reversible muscle symptoms.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Elevated LDL cholesterol is a well-established, modifiable risk factor for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Statins are the first-line pharmacological therapy for lowering LDL cholesterol and have been studied extensively in both primary and secondary prevention.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: In this synthetic prospective cohort, 2,400 adults aged 45 to 65 years with moderate cardiovascular risk and no prior statin use were started on a moderate-intensity statin. Fasting lipid panels were measured at baseline and at twelve weeks.
Adherence was assessed by pill counts, and adverse events were recorded at each visit. The primary outcome was the percentage change in LDL cholesterol from baseline.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Mean LDL cholesterol fell from 158 mg/dL at baseline to 103 mg/dL at twelve weeks, a reduction of approximately 35 percent. Muscle-related symptoms were reported by 6 percent of participants and resolved after dose adjustment in nearly all cases.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: These synthetic findings are consistent with the expected class effect of statins on LDL cholesterol. The reduction observed supports the use of moderate-intensity statins in adults at moderate cardiovascular risk, with routine monitoring for muscle symptoms.
